perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party agrees to return all Confidential Information to Disclosing Party within 15 days of written demand by Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party agrees to return all Confidential Information to Disclosing Party within 5 days of written demand by Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the return period to 5 days may not be reasonable, particularly if it involves significant effort to gather and return the information. This may violate Oregon's implied duty of good faith and fair dealing if it makes compliance unreasonably difficult. This change introduces a requirement that could be seen as unfairly short under Oregon law, especially for complex confidential data.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Oregon's Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: While Oregon does not have a specific statute mandating a certain return period for confidential information, reducing the timeframe to 5 days could be a violation of the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, especially if compliance is unduly burdensome. The implied duty requires parties to act in a way that does not destroy the right of the other party to receive the fruits of the contract. A very short deadline could be seen as a breach of this duty.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ORS 71.3020 – Variation by agreement ORS 71.3020 Variation by agreement Text Annotations (1) Except as otherwise provided in subsection (2) of this section or elsewhere in the Uniform Commercial Code, the effect of provisions of the Uniform Commercial Code may be varied by agreement. (2) The obligations of good faith, diligence, reasonableness and care prescribed by the Uniform Commercial Code may not be disclaimed by agreement. The parties, by agreement, may determine the standards by which the performance of those obligations is to be measured if those standards are not manifestly unreasonable. Whenever the Uniform Commercial Code requires an action to be taken within a reasonable time, a time that is not manifestly unreasonable may be fixed by agreement. (3) The presence in certain provisions of the Uniform Commercial Code of the phrase “unless otherwise agreed,” or words of similar import, does not imply that the effect of other provisions may not be varied by agreement under this section. [2009 c.181 §15] Note: See note under 71.3010 (Territorial applicability) . Source: Section 71.3020 — Variation by agreement , https://www.­oregonlegislature.­gov/bills_laws/ors/ors071.­html (accessed May 26, 2025). 71.1010 Short titles 71.1020 Scope of chapter 71.1030 Construction to promote purposes and policies 71.1040 Construction against implied repeal 71.1050 Severability 71.1060 Use of singular and plural 71.1070 Captions 71.1080 Relation to Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act 71.2010 General definitions 71.2020 Notice 71.2030 Lease distinguished from security interest 71.2040 Value 71.2050 Reasonable time 71.2060 Presumptions 71.3010 Territorial applicability 71.3020 Variation by agreement 71.3030 Course of performance, course of dealing and usage of trade 71.3040 Obligation of good faith 71.3050 Remedies to be liberally administered 71.3060 Waiver or renunciation of claim or right after breach 71.3070 Prima facie evidence by third-party documents 71.3080 Pe

perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the return period of confidential information from 15 days to 5 days. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation state this may violate Oregon's implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, especially if compliance is unduly burdensome. ORS 71.3020(2) states that 'The obligations of good faith, diligence, reasonableness and care prescribed by the Uniform Commercial Code may not be disclaimed by agreement...Whenever the Uniform Commercial Code requires an action to be taken within a reasonable time, a time that is not manifestly unreasonable may be fixed by agreement.' By reducing the time to 5 days, this might be considered manifestly unreasonable and a violation of good faith, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any controversy or claim arising out of or relating to this agreement, or the breach thereof, shall be resolved by arbitration conducted by the Commercial Division of the American Arbitration Asso ciation and in accordance with the rules thereof, or in any other convenient forum agreed to in writing by the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: Any controversy or claim arising out of or relating to this agreement, or the breach thereof, shall be resolved by mandatory arbitration, waiving all rights to class action or jury trial. The decision of the arbitrator shall be final and non-appealable, regardless of errors of law or fact.
perturbation.explanation: Forcing mandatory arbitration with a waiver of class action and jury trial rights, along with a non-appealable decision even for errors of law, could be deemed unconscionable or against public policy, particularly concerning claims involving federal or state laws where the right to a jury trial is protected, particularly if there is a power imbalance between the parties. This is relevant under the FAA, as state contract law can invalidate arbitration agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Federal Arbitration Act allows arbitration agreements but they cannot be unconscionable. By explicitly waiving all appeal rights, even for errors of law, the modified text attempts to shield the arbitration process from judicial review, which might be considered an attempt to circumvent legal standards and therefore be deemed unconscionable under state contract law principles applicable through the FAA.

Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising ou
perturbation.justification: The modified text mandates arbitration, waiving class action and jury trial rights, and makes the arbitrator's decision final even for errors of law. This contradicts the principle that arbitration agreements must not be unconscionable. The Law Explanation states the waiver of appeal rights attempts to shield the arbitration process from judicial review and could be unconscionable. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 9 USC 2, affirms the validity and enforceability of arbitration agreements, but this is implicitly limited by the condition that the agreement is not unconscionable under applicable state law. The inclusion of the clause preventing appeals for errors of law creates an unconscionable situation as it shields the decision from judicial review.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement and all questions relating to its validity, interpretation, performance and enforcement (including, without limitation, provisions concerning limitations of actions), shall be governed by and cons trued in accordance with the laws of the State Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Agreement Page 4 of Oregon, notwithstanding any conflict-of -laws doctrines of such state or other jurisdiction to the contrary, and without th e aid of any canon, custom or rule of law requiring construction against the draftsman.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement and all questions relating to its validity, interpretation, performance and enforcement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Nevada, regardless of the location of the parties or the subject matter of the agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Selecting Nevada law, even though the parties and subject matter may be based in Oregon, might violate Oregon's conflict of laws principles if Nevada law provides significantly less protection for confidential information or has statutes of limitations that are drastically different. This undermines the reasonable expectations of the parties. For example, if Oregon has a longer statute of limitations for trade secret misappropriation, selecting Nevada law to shorten this period may be unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Oregon's conflict of laws principles, as generally reflected in the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187, allow parties to choose the governing law, but this choice is not absolute. If the chosen state (Nevada) has no substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction and its law contradicts a fundamental policy of Oregon, the choice of law provision may be disregarded. This alteration could create such a conflict.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies Oregon law, while the changed text specifies Nevada law, potentially undermining Oregon's conflict of laws principles. According to the Law Explanation, Oregon's conflict of laws principles, based on Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187, allow parties to choose the governing law, but this choice isn't absolute. If Nevada has no substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction and its law contradicts a fundamental policy of Oregon, the choice of law provision may be disregarded. The Perturbation Explanation adds that this is especially critical if Nevada law provides less protection or has differing statutes of limitations. The absence of helpful information in the scraped snippets does not invalidate the logical contradiction created by this change in governing law.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party agrees to return all Confidential Information to Disclosing Party within 15 days of written demand by Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party's obligation to return Confidential Information is nullified if the Receiving Party can demonstrate any potential claim, however tenuous, against the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Stating that the receiving party is not obligated to return the confidential information if they can demonstrate a potential claim is not permissible. This change introduces a scenario that violates the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, and is likely unenforceable under Oregon contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Oregon's Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: Oregon's implied duty of good faith and fair dealing prevents parties from acting in ways that frustrate the other party's ability to receive the benefits of the contract. Allowing the receiving party to avoid returning confidential information based on any potential claim, however weak, effectively nullifies a key provision of the NDA and violates this duty. Such a clause would be unlikely to be enforced by Oregon courts.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ORS 71.3020 – Variation by agreement ORS 71.3020 Variation by agreement Text Annotations (1) Except as otherwise provided in subsection (2) of this section or elsewhere in the Uniform Commercial Code, the effect of provisions of the Uniform Commercial Code may be varied by agreement. (2) The obligations of good faith, diligence, reasonableness and care prescribed by the Uniform Commercial Code may not be disclaimed by agreement. The parties, by agreement, may determine the standards by which the performance of those obligations is to be measured if those standards are not manifestly unreasonable. Whenever the Uniform Commercial Code requires an action to be taken within a reasonable time, a time that is not manifestly unreasonable may be fixed by agreement. (3) The presence in certain provisions of the Uniform Commercial Code of the phrase “unless otherwise agreed,” or words of similar import, does not imply that the effect of other provisions may not be varied by agreement under this section. [2009 c.181 §15] Note: See note under 71.3010 (Territorial applicability) . perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing in contract law, as the Receiving Party's obligation to return Confidential Information is nullified if they can demonstrate a potential claim. This undermines the Disclosing Party's ability to receive the benefits of the contract. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 indicates a 'Page Not Found,' Scraped Law Snippet 1, ORS 71.3020, subsection (2), states that 'The obligations of good faith... prescribed by the Uniform Commercial Code may not be disclaimed by agreement.' The altered contract language effectively disclaims the obligation to return confidential information, contingent on a 'tenuous' claim, thus creating a contradiction with the principle of good faith.

perturbation.original_text: All information provided by the Disclosing Party shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: All information provided by the Disclosing Party shall become the joint property of both parties upon disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Changing ownership of the disclosed information from the Disclosing Party to joint ownership directly contradicts the purpose of a non-disclosure agreement, which is to protect the Disclosing Party's proprietary information. This would effectively transfer intellectual property rights without proper assignment, and could violate trade secret laws. This is because under Oregon law, trade secrets are protected, and this clause would eliminate the basis for that protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Oregon Trade Secrets Act protects trade secrets from misappropriation. By making the confidential information joint property, it removes a key element of trade secret protection – that the information belongs solely to the Disclosing Party. This would mean that the 'Receiving Party' could then disclose the trade secrets without legal consequence as they now co-own them.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ORS 646.461 – Definitions for ORS 646.461 to 646.475 ORS 646.461 Definitions for ORS 646.461 to 646.475 Text Annotations As used in ORS 646.461 (Definitions for ORS 646.461 to 646.475) to 646.475 (Application and construction of ORS 646.461 to 646.475) , unless the context otherwise requires: (1) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering and independent development alone shall not be considered improper means. (2) “Misappropriation” means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (c) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who, before a material change of position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake; or (d) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person, who at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use. (3) “Person” means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) “Trade secret” means information, including a drawing, cost data, customer list, formula, pattern, compilation, pro
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the purpose of maintaining trade secret protection under Oregon law. By changing the ownership of information from the Disclosing Party to joint ownership, it removes the Disclosing Party's exclusive rights to the trade secret, which is a fundamental requirement for trade secret protection. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines "Misappropriation" as the disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent. The changed text effectively gives implied consent to the Receiving Party, as they are now co-owners, thereby eliminating the possibility of misappropriation and undermining the protection afforded by the Oregon Trade Secrets Act. The change creates a direct contradiction because the law protects information owned solely by one party, but the modified contract creates joint ownership, undermining this protection.
